mappings.rationale: A core text on how cities organize their basic engineering services (A), urban transportation systems (A.3), and recreational facilities (B.2).
Water supply and sanitation are the engineering backbone of urban life, directly relevant to A.1 (water-supply systems) and A.2 (sanitation systems).
Suburban sprawl creates distinct technological challenges for transportation (A.3) and infrastructure delivery that differ fundamentally from dense urban cores.
Examines market-based instruments—taxes, cap-and-trade, subsidies—for controlling air, water, and land pollution (C.1) in urban settings.
Pollution control (C.1) is as much a political contest as a technical one, and this volume explores the ideologies and movements behind urban environmental regulation.
Provides the legal framework for C (controlling air, water, and land pollution), explaining statutes, enforcement, and liability in urban communities.
Clean water (A.1) and sanitation (A.2) were the great public health triumphs of the nineteenth century, and this volume traces how that infrastructure saved millions of lives.
Explores crime patterns and policing methods in urban communities, directly relevant to B.1.b (crime control technology).
Forensic evidence, examination, and identification techniques are central to B.1.b (crime control technology) in modern cities.
Understanding water's chemistry is fundamental to A.1 (water-supply systems), from purification and treatment to distribution in urban communities.
Parks and recreational facilities (B.2) are critical urban infrastructure, and this volume examines their design, construction, and maintenance.
Addresses how cities can build sustainable infrastructure and manage pollution (C), balancing growth with environmental responsibility.
Cities are major contributors to greenhouse gas emissions, making this essential for understanding C.1 (control of air pollution) at the urban scale.
Electric power (A.5) is a basic engineering service of the city, and this volume explains how electricity is generated, transmitted, and distributed.
